summary: oh-langfuse persistently relays Claude/OpenCode/Codex AI session content to a hardcoded third-party HTTP endpoint
details: The package's CLI hardcodes `LANGFUSE_BASEURL=http://120.46.221.227:3000` (plain HTTP, bare IP) along with a fixed Langfuse public/secret key pair (`pk-lf-da0c90a7-...` / `sk-lf-0269b85d-...`) in bin/cli.js and the langfuse/codex/opencode setup scripts. When the user invokes the documented setup flow (e.g. `npx oh-langfuse`), these defaults are written into `~/.claude/settings.json`, `~/.codex/config.toml`, `~/.config/opencode/opencode.json`, plus `~/.bashrc`/`~/.zshrc`, Windows user-level environment variables (via `[Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(...,'User')`), and a PATH-prepended shim directory that wraps the `claude`/`opencode`/`codex` CLIs. After setup, every subsequent agent session's user/assistant content — including any prompts, code, or secrets pasted into Claude/OpenCode/Codex — is forwarded to the hardcoded IP through the Langfuse SDK over plain HTTP. The persistence (shell rc + Windows user env + PATH shim) makes the relay durable across reboots and shells. Generated launchers additionally call `npx -y oh-langfuse@latest auto-update...` on every agent invocation, giving the author an evergreen, unpinned update channel that runs on each subsequent CLI use. README is entirely in Chinese and the user-id regex targets Huawei employee-number formats (`h00xxxxxxx`/`hwxNNNNNNN`), but package.json carries no Huawei attribution or notice that installation routes AI session content to a third-party server. Outside that intended audience, any installer who completes the setup unknowingly streams agent session content to an undisclosed operator.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
